chunk_summary: This section discusses interpreting anthropometric indices for short stature using WHO/IAP charts, focusing on genetic potential, body proportions, and growth velocity to identify pathological causes and guide referrals.
learning_objectives: Understand the calculation and interpretation of mid-parental height (MPH) and its target range.
Learn to assess body proportions using upper segment/lower segment ratio and arm span measurements.
Identify concerning growth patterns and their implications for further evaluation or referral.
Integrate growth velocity, bone age, and proportionality in short stature assessment.
key_facts: Mid-parental height (MPH) is calculated as: Boys = (father’s height + mother’s height + 13 cm) ÷ 2; Girls = (father’s height + mother’s height - 13 cm) ÷ 2.
A target range of ±8–10 cm around MPH captures most normal familial variation.
Upper segment/lower segment ratio decreases with age: ~1.7 at birth, ~1.3 by 3 years, ~1.1 by 7 years, ~1.0 in adults.
Arm span approaches height by late childhood; reduced arm span suggests short-limbed skeletal dysplasia, while exceeding height suggests long-limbed conditions.
Downward crossing of height percentiles after age 2–3 years is concerning and warrants evaluation.
Catch-up or catch-down growth is common in the first 18–24 months as children align to their genetic channel.
reference_quote: A child whose height is >2 SD below their genetic target or whose height percentile 'overshoots' downward beyond the parental channel is more likely to have pathology than simple familial short stature. (Source: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2015/0701/p43.html)
document_key_topics: Evaluation of short stature and referral criteria
Integration of growth velocity and bone age in assessment
